header.title: Daily FI Tape
header.as_of_note: NY close briefing; Asia handoff is shown separately.
header.latest_update_cutoff: 2026-06-05 close plus the next Taipei handoff

bullets: UST2Y rose 12 bp to 4.17%, UST5Y rose 11 bp to 4.29%, and the curve flattened as Fed-hike pricing returned.
Real yields, the dollar, gold, VIX, and the semiconductor selloff all confirmed a rates-led risk shock rather than a benign growth surprise.
Credit access remained open for stronger structures, but EM and refinancing stories showed a higher all-in-yield hurdle.
overall_summary.ny_close_causal_narrative: Strong payrolls drove a front-end-led rates selloff, higher real yields, dollar strength, technology-led equity weakness, and selective credit pressure.

top_drivers.title: Payrolls reset Fed pricing
top_drivers.short_summary: A stronger labor print moved the policy-sensitive curve higher and pulled hike pricing back into the center of the tape.
top_drivers.detail: May job growth near 172,000 with unemployment around 4.3% pushed UST2Y up 12 bp and UST5Y up 11 bp, while swaps priced a meaningful chance of Fed tightening by year-end.

top_drivers.title: Real-yield shock hit risk assets
top_drivers.short_summary: Higher real yields pressured growth equities, gold, and credit beta even though crude fell on the day.
top_drivers.detail: TIPS10Y rose 8 bp, gold fell 3.10%, SOX dropped 10.26%, VIX jumped nearly 40%, and the dollar gained 0.66%, creating a broad real-rate confirmation of the Treasury selloff.

top_drivers.title: Credit stayed open but selective
top_drivers.short_summary: Primary demand existed for quality structures, while weaker and EM borrowers remained exposed to refinancing and policy credibility risk.
top_drivers.detail: Google-linked prepaid energy demand and AI infrastructure financing showed access, but Senegal coupon timing, Raizen restructuring, and weaker credit beta kept the tone cautious.

latest_asia_premarket.summary: Separately, checked Asia/pre-market pricing before the Taipei handoff shows 10Y Treasury note futures 109.08 (-0.24%); 30Y Treasury bond futures 111.75 (-0.45%); S&P 500 futures 7,400 (0%); Nasdaq 100 futures 29,026 (0%); WTI crude futures 90.54 (-0%).

items.title: 10Y Treasury note futures
items.detail: 109.08, -0.24% from the prior close. Treasury futures give the cleanest early read on whether the duration bid held after the NY close

items.title: 30Y Treasury bond futures
items.detail: 111.75, -0.45% from the prior close. Long-bond futures help separate a persistent long-end bid from a one-session cash-market move

items.title: S&P 500 futures
items.detail: 7,400, 0% from the prior close. Equity futures show whether the overnight handoff confirmed or faded the NY risk tone

items.title: Nasdaq 100 futures
items.detail: 29,026, 0% from the prior close. Nasdaq futures are the early check on whether growth and technology beta stabilized

items.title: WTI crude futures
items.detail: 90.54, -0% from the prior close. WTI futures test whether the oil channel was still easing inflation pressure by the Taipei handoff

watch_events.title: Warsh Fed communication
watch_events.why_it_matters: Markets had moved toward hike pricing; the first Warsh-led meeting would test whether the Fed validates or resists that repricing.

watch_events.title: AI-linked credit pricing
watch_events.why_it_matters: Large AI infrastructure financing remained open, but equity weakness raised questions about collateral value and refinancing demand.

watch_events.title: Oil and real-yield follow-through
watch_events.why_it_matters: Lower crude helped contain breakevens, but elevated real yields kept pressure on gold, equities, and duration credit.

cross_asset_cards.title: Oil
cross_asset_cards.summary: WTI was -2.69% on the day and +3.64% over one week; Brent moved in the same direction, pulling inflation compensation lower and reinforcing Treasury demand.

cross_asset_cards.title: Equities
cross_asset_cards.summary: SPX was -2.64%, NDX -4.77%, and SOX -10.3%; the equity tape leaned tech-beta downside rather than broad risk-appetite confirmation.

cross_asset_cards.title: Credit
cross_asset_cards.summary: LQD was -0.62%, HYG -0.5%, and EMB -0.73%; these are ETF total returns, not direct OAS or CDX. With Treasury yields lower, duration should have helped bond ETF prices, so flat or negative HY/EM returns argue for weak spread confirmation rather than a broad credit rally.

cross_asset_cards.title: Dollar / gold
cross_asset_cards.summary: DXY was +0.66% while gold was -3.1%, consistent with lower yields and limited haven stress.

long_thesis.english_markdown:
## Opening fixed-income thesis

June 5 was a classic front-end-led repricing day, but the important point is broader than the phrase “Treasuries sold off.” The market absorbed a payroll print strong enough to challenge the idea that policy was already restrictive, then repriced the Fed path, the dollar, equities, gold, credit beta, and parts of global duration around that same inflation-control problem. UST2Y finished near 4.17%, up 12 bp, UST5Y near 4.29%, up 11 bp, UST10Y near 4.55%, up 8 bp, and UST30Y near 5.01%, up 4 bp. The curve flattened because the front and belly carried the policy shock: 2s10s ended near 38 bp, 4 bp flatter on the day, while 2s30s compressed to about 84 bp, 8 bp flatter. That is not a benign duration adjustment. It is a market saying that the hurdle for easier policy moved higher after the labor data, while long-end inflation compensation did not need to rise much for real yields to reprice.

The payroll details gave the repricing a direct macro anchor. May job growth came in around 172,000 and the unemployment rate held near 4.3%, a combination that reduced the market’s willingness to treat slowing growth as the dominant fixed-income input. Fed hike pricing moved materially: swaps were described as pricing a quarter-point increase by December, with October also drawing meaningful probability. That mattered because the Fed leadership transition under Kevin Warsh was already a live market issue. The June 16-17 meeting was approaching, and the easy communication step was no longer how to preserve optionality around cuts; it was how forcefully to validate the market’s renewed inflation concern without turning a data-dependent stance into panic.

Cross-asset confirmation was severe. The S&P 500 fell 2.64%, the Nasdaq 100 fell 4.77%, and the SOX index dropped 10.26%. VIX rose nearly 40%. The dollar rose 0.66%, and gold fell 3.10%. Those moves fit a real-rate and risk-premium shock: the stronger dollar and weaker gold were consistent with higher real yields, while the semiconductor collapse showed that the equity market was not simply discounting stronger nominal growth. It was repricing long-duration growth assets against a higher discount-rate path. Oil did not rescue the inflation story on the day, with WTI down 2.69% and Brent down 2.04%, but that did not make the bond move dovish. The inflation risk in the reading set was tied to the prior oil shock from the Iran conflict and to the Fed’s reaction function, not to a fresh crude spike at the close.

Credit was open but not clean. LQD fell 0.62%, HYG fell 0.50%, JNK fell 0.44%, and EMB fell 0.73%. Because those are total-return prices, they do not by themselves prove spread widening. Still, the combination of sharply higher front-end yields, falling equities, a higher dollar, and weaker EM debt is hard to square with broad spread compression. The better reading is that primary markets and borrower access were still functioning for stronger or well-structured borrowers, while weaker credits and refinancing stories remained vulnerable to higher all-in yields. That distinction is the day’s credit message: not a closed market, but a more discriminating one.

## Rates, curve, and Fed pricing

The rates move was driven by the policy-sensitive part of the curve. UST2Y up 12 bp and UST5Y up 11 bp against UST30Y up 4 bp tells a straightforward story: the market shifted from a long-end term-premium debate into a front-end Fed-path repricing. The 2s10s flattening to roughly 38 bp and 2s30s flattening to about 84 bp are consistent with a market bringing hikes forward or taking cuts out, rather than one demanding a pure long-end supply concession. The real-rate channel was also clear. TIPS10Y finished around 2.19%, up 8 bp, while 10Y breakevens were roughly 236 bp and essentially unchanged on the day. If nominal 10s rose by about 8 bp and breakevens did not move, the pressure came through real yields.

That is why the payroll report mattered more than the headline equity decline. A 172,000 payroll gain with unemployment steady near 4.3% kept the Fed’s focus on inflation. It also strengthened the market’s view that the economy was not yet weak enough to offset the inflation shock that had followed the Iran-driven oil move earlier in the year. The reading set framed the post-February shift as a reversal of rate-cut expectations after oil and inflation risks destroyed the easier-policy narrative. June 5 extended that shift. The bond market was not merely reacting to one data release; it was updating the probability that the Fed under Warsh would have to demonstrate anti-inflation credibility quickly.

The Fed pricing details make that point concrete. A December hike became a central market scenario, with the October meeting carrying a substantial probability. That was a very different setup from a market waiting for confirmation of easing. It also explains why gold fell and the dollar rose. If higher nominal yields are being driven by higher expected real policy rates, gold should struggle even when geopolitical risk remains present. Dollar strength also fits a resilient-US and higher-real-rate frame. The local dollar move was not a side note; it was part of the same Fed repricing.

The international rate data reinforced the direction. Canadian bonds sold off after Canada added 87,800 jobs in May, far above expectations, and the Canadian two-year yield rose around 10 bp. That parallel mattered because it reduced the chance that the U.S. payroll response was an isolated domestic overreaction. It suggested a broader reassessment of labor resilience and central-bank room to stay tight. Colombian inflation also moved higher, with annual inflation accelerating to 5.84% from 5.68%, keeping EM policy risk pointed toward caution. The common thread was that the market was receiving more evidence of sticky inflation pressure and resilient labor markets than of a clean disinflationary slowdown.

The curve therefore carried an important message. Long-end yields rose, but not enough to make the day primarily a term-premium story. The front end and belly did the work. That matters for the rest of the report because it changes how to read every other asset class. Credit weakness was not just credit fear; it reflected the all-in yield shock. Equity weakness was not just growth pessimism; it reflected discount-rate pressure. Gold weakness was not just lower haven demand; it reflected higher real yields. The policy-sensitive curve move was the transmission channel.

## Supply, term premium, and liquidity

Direct Treasury supply evidence was limited on June 5, so the supply read stays narrower than the rates section. The day’s Treasury move does not prove anything by itself about auction demand, dealer balance-sheet capacity, or the government’s financing capacity. The supported supply and liquidity evidence was instead in the funding behavior of corporates, infrastructure borrowers, and public-sector borrowers. Those markets showed that capital was still available, but not uniformly cheap and not equally available to every issuer.

The most important funding theme was the scale of AI-related capital demand. A $35 billion debt package for Anthropic showed that private-credit and structured debt markets were being asked to fund infrastructure at a size that normally belongs in sovereign or megacap corporate discussions. The package was tied to data-center and chip spending, and the broader reading set noted that technology companies were tapping multiple corners of credit markets to finance AI capacity. That does not create an immediate Treasury supply claim, but it does matter for fixed income because it puts future refinancing supply, collateral quality, and lender concentration into the same conversation as rates. If AI infrastructure remains a capital-intensive story, credit markets will have to decide whether cash-flow visibility is strong enough to absorb very large financing needs at higher base rates.

Municipal and structured funding also looked open. A Google-linked prepaid energy transaction of roughly $1 billion drew strong demand, and the largest tranche priced around 95 bp over the benchmark before trading tighter. That is a useful contrast with the risk-off tone in equities. Strong sponsorship and a familiar structure still attracted money, even as the Treasury market repriced higher. This is why the right market conclusion is not “funding shut.” It is that high-quality, well-sponsored issuance could clear, while duration-heavy or lower-quality borrowers faced a higher all-in-yield hurdle.

The weaker side of liquidity came through EM and distressed-credit examples. Senegal paid foreign-currency coupons early before IMF talks, including euro- and dollar-denominated payments that were not due until the following week. Early payment can calm immediate default anxiety, but it also underscores that investors were focused on program credibility and debt-service capacity. Raizen reached an out-of-court restructuring covering about 65 billion reais of obligations, with a large equity conversion and the rest rolling into new debt. Those facts belong in the fixed-income tape because they show what the high-rate environment does to weaker balance sheets: it does not always close the primary market, but it makes liability management and debt exchanges a more central feature of the credit landscape.

Term premium was present, but it was not the dominant close driver. UST30Y rose only 4 bp against the much larger move in 2s and 5s. If investors were demanding a broad long-end supply concession, the long bond would normally carry more of the day. Instead, the curve flattened and real yields rose, which puts the emphasis on the Fed reaction function. Supply and liquidity therefore sit behind the main story: they shape how borrowers handle the new rate level, but they did not explain the first-order Treasury move.

China’s capital-flow story also belongs in the credit discussion because it affects offshore wealth, Hong Kong-linked balance sheets, and the availability of risk capital for dollar assets. Beijing’s crackdown targeted overseas brokers and wealthy individuals, with levies of up to 20% on investment gains and potential penalties for overdue payments. Citic Securities estimated that the clampdown could affect as much as HK$250 billion of assets, while separate estimates put resident outflows near $807 billion last year. That is not a direct U.S. rates driver, but it is relevant to EM and Asian credit appetite: when capital mobility is restricted, offshore liquidity can become less reliable at the same time that dollar rates are rising.

## Credit conditions

Credit conditions were mixed in a way that matters more than the ETF tape alone. LQD, HYG, JNK, and EMB all fell, but those losses include duration and rates effects. The more useful conclusion is that the day’s credit market faced a tougher all-in-yield backdrop while still allowing differentiated access. High-quality or strategically important borrowers could still raise money; more fragile credits needed concessions, liability management, or official-program credibility.

The investment-grade and municipal examples leaned constructive. The Google-linked prepaid energy deal showed that investors would still fund a structure with strong sponsorship and clear cash-flow mechanics. Secondary tightening after pricing suggested that money was not absent from high-quality fixed income. The AI infrastructure complex sent a different signal. The Anthropic financing was enormous, and it highlighted both the depth of private credit and the risk that AI capital expenditure becomes a recurring source of debt supply. That is not automatically negative credit news. It can be a sign that lenders are comfortable funding high-growth infrastructure. But after a day when SOX fell more than 10%, the market could no longer treat AI-linked credit as insulated from equity repricing. If the equity market questions AI valuations, lenders must re-underwrite terminal demand, collateral values, and refinancing options.

The spread-risk side was visible in emerging-market and distressed examples. Senegal’s early coupon payment showed a borrower trying to preserve credibility before IMF negotiations. That can support near-term bond prices, but it also confirms that investors were focused on debt sustainability rather than simply coupon status. Raizen’s restructuring showed that commodity-linked or leveraged borrowers were not all being protected by the broader inflation regime. A debt exchange that converts a large portion into equity is a reminder that higher nominal growth does not protect every balance sheet when leverage, currency exposure, or earnings volatility is high.

The macro overlay made credit harder. Strong payrolls supported the idea that companies could still generate revenue, but they also raised the discount rate and refinancing cost. That is the central credit tension. A resilient economy can delay defaults, but higher real yields make future refinancing more expensive and equity volatility reduces the cushion for lower-rated borrowers. The June 5 equity selloff was concentrated in long-duration technology, but the credit implication extended beyond tech: if policy rates stay high or move higher, borrowers that rely on optimistic cash-flow growth become more vulnerable.

The day’s credit conclusion is therefore selective caution. It was not a universal credit break. It was also not a clean risk-on market. Strong borrowers cleared funding. AI-related financing still found lenders. EM and distressed borrowers remained under scrutiny. Credit ETFs weakened alongside rates and equities. That mix supports a view of wider risk compensation at the margin, even if a precise OAS move is not available in the input set.

The gold move made the real-yield story more visible. Bullion fell enough to erase its year-to-date gain intraday after payrolls, with higher yields and a stronger dollar doing most of the damage. That matters because gold normally benefits from geopolitical risk; on June 5, the rates shock overpowered the haven bid. The same pattern appeared in equities. Technology and semiconductor shares did not merely pause; they repriced violently as the discount-rate path moved higher. That cross-asset shape strengthens the conclusion that June 5 was a real-rate shock first and a growth scare second.

Canada provided the cleanest non-U.S. developed-market rates confirmation, but Colombia and China added EM context. Colombian inflation accelerated on fuel, rents, food, and restaurants, and the central bank had already paused at a high 11.25% policy rate despite political pressure. China’s capital-control campaign, meanwhile, pointed to a different form of policy stress: authorities were trying to restrain outflows just as global investors were demanding more compensation for dollar risk. Those channels did not all move Treasuries at the close, but they explain why EM hard-currency credit underperformed and why dollar strength mattered.

## Global duration, EM hard-currency credit, and cross-market confirmation

The global-duration input set was thinner than the U.S. and credit evidence, so the global read rests on confirmed non-U.S. signals rather than unsupported claims. Canada was the clearest developed-market confirmation. Its bond market sold off after a much stronger jobs report, with the two-year yield up around 10 bp. That made the U.S. payroll reaction look less isolated. When multiple labor markets challenge central-bank easing, global duration cannot price the Fed as the only tightening risk.

EM and commodity-linked signals added a more complicated layer. Colombian inflation accelerated to 5.84%, creating pressure for policymakers to consider tighter settings. China’s capital-outflow controls pointed to persistent stress in cross-border capital behavior, with estimates of very large resident outflows and significant Hong Kong assets potentially affected by tax enforcement. Those facts do not map mechanically into Treasury yields, but they matter for EM hard-currency credit because they describe an environment where domestic policy constraints, capital mobility, and dollar funding remain sensitive.

Cross-asset confirmation was strong. The dollar rose, gold fell, VIX spiked, and equities sold off hard. That combination is consistent with a rates-led tightening shock rather than a soft-landing rally. The SOX decline was especially important because it turned AI from a financing-growth theme into a volatility amplifier. If the market is simultaneously funding AI infrastructure at large scale and repricing AI equities lower, the credit market has to think harder about duration, collateral value, and terminal demand. That is why the technology selloff belongs in a fixed-income tape.

Oil was a partial contradiction only at the daily close. WTI and Brent fell on the day, but the broader reading set still tied the year’s policy repricing to the earlier Iran-related oil shock and to concerns that crude could remain high even under a diplomatic path. The correct interpretation is that crude did not add new inflation pressure on June 5, while the prior oil shock continued to influence the Fed reaction function. Inflation compensation confirmed that nuance: 10Y breakevens were roughly unchanged, while real yields drove nominal yields higher.

The global close therefore confirmed the U.S. rates story through different channels. Canada confirmed labor-driven rate risk. EM stories confirmed policy and funding constraints. The dollar, gold, and equities confirmed real-rate pressure. Oil did not drive the day, but it remained a background constraint on central-bank easing. That is enough for a global fixed-income read, even with thinner direct global-duration coverage than on some other days.

A fourth watchpoint was whether offshore and EM credit would confirm the dollar shock. Senegal’s early coupon payment helped near-term optics, but it did not remove the IMF-program question. Colombia’s inflation acceleration raised the possibility that Latin American easing cycles could stall. China’s capital-flow restrictions made offshore liquidity less straightforward. Those are different stories, but the fixed-income implication is common: if the dollar remains firm and U.S. real yields stay high, credit markets outside the U.S. will need either stronger local policy credibility or wider compensation.

## Market implications and watchpoints

The first watchpoint after June 5 was whether Fed communication would validate or resist the hike pricing. With the first Warsh-led meeting approaching, the market had already moved toward a higher-for-longer or outright-tightening stance. If Fed officials accepted the payroll signal and emphasized inflation credibility, the front end could keep pressure on the curve. If they pushed back against hike pricing, the market would need to decide whether the labor data was strong enough to hold the front end high without explicit Fed endorsement.

The second watchpoint was whether the equity shock would feed credit spreads. On June 5, the ETF tape was negative but contaminated by rates duration. The cleaner test would be whether primary-market concessions widened, whether AI infrastructure borrowers needed materially higher coupons, and whether EM borrowers such as Senegal or distressed commodity-linked issuers faced renewed pressure. A market can absorb one risk-off equity day. It becomes a credit event only if funding terms deteriorate across issuers or if weaker borrowers lose access.

The third watchpoint was the real-yield channel. With TIPS10Y up 8 bp and gold down sharply, the market had repriced real rates in a way that could keep pressuring long-duration assets. If real yields stayed high while breakevens remained stable or lower, growth equities and duration credit would remain vulnerable even without another oil spike. If oil rose again and breakevens followed, the market would face a different problem: inflation compensation and real yields both moving against risk assets.

The final watchpoint was global confirmation. Canada already moved in the same direction as the U.S. EM inflation and capital-flow stories were not yet first-order drivers of Treasuries, but they could become important if dollar strength persisted. A stronger dollar plus higher U.S. real yields is rarely benign for EM hard-currency credit. June 5 left the fixed-income market with a clear message: the path of least resistance shifted toward tighter policy pricing, but the durability of that move depended on Fed validation, credit-market absorption, and whether risk assets could stabilize after the rates shock.

methodology_note: Original market synthesis from the date-specific fixed-income reading set and checked market levels.
